data.title: Maersk leads container shippers down as freight rates plunge to nearly two-year lows
data.description:
Shipping companies A.P. Moller-Maersk (OTCPK:AMKBY) (OTCPK:AMKAF) and Hapag-Lloyd (OTCPK:HPGLY) (OTCPK:HLAGF) -6.6% and -7.5%, respectively, in Friday's trading as container freight rates on major routes out of Shanghai sink to levels last seen before Red Sea diversions began in late 2023.

The Shanghai Containerized Freight Index has plunged more than 14% in the past week, the largest weekly percentage decline in nine years. according to the Shanghai Shipping Exchange.

The week's biggest declines have occurred on the TransPacific trade: Spot rates from Shanghai to the U.S. West Coast plummeted 31% to $1,646/feu, and rates from Shanghai to the U.S. East Coast plunged 23% to $2,557/feu.

Rates from Asia to Europe also continue to decline, with rates to north Europe and the Mediterranean both down more than 10% to $1,052/teu and $1,638/teu, respectively.

"Far East-to-U.S. west coast rates are now down 56% from its recent peak reached in week 23 and 61% down from week 1," J.P. Morgan analysts said, and maritime research consultancy Drewry said in a note that its world container index, which tracks freight rates for 40 ft containers on eight major shipping routes, shows the major Trans Pacific and Asia-Europe routes are now "aligned in a downwards trajectory."

The declines come ahead of Golden Week that traditionally marks the start of shipping's slow season, which analysts said offer a bleak prospect for rates in the coming weeks.

data.title: BAM-backed Rockpoint Gas Storage files for IPO in Canada - report
data.description:
Rockpoint Gas Storage, the North American natural gas storage platform backed by Brookfield Asset Management (NYSE:BAM), has filed for a Canadian initial public offering, according to a media report on Friday.

The Toronto listing will comprise new shares and existing stock, Bloomberg reported, citing a preliminary prospectus. The IPO price and number of shares offered will be disclosed in subsequent filings.

Brookfield (NYSE:BAM) will maintain control after the offering, the article said, keeping some 60% of the operating units and all the Class B shares. Rockpoint was incorporated this year to buy about 40% of two controlled operating partnerships controlled by Brookfield.

The filing showed that Rockpoint's net income and revenue were $209.4M and $415.3M, respectively, in the fiscal year ended March 31. That compares with $253.9M of net income and $348.6M of revenue in the prior 12 months.

data.title: Kohl's added to Citi's 90 day positive catalyst watch
data.description:
Citi Research has placed Kohl’s Corporation (NYSE:KSS) on Positive Catalyst Watch as improved comparable store sales comparisons in the second half of this year and an acceleration in web traffic suggests the consensus expectations for the department store are “overly conservative.”

“While the second half is likely to present macro challenges, and longer-term secular issues remain, we believe that near-term comp upside combined with the company’s high short-interest (~30%) should move the stock higher in the near term,” Citi analyst Lejuez writes in Friday’s note to clients.

Lejuez cites Citi’s analysis which indicates H2 comparisons are ~325 basis points easier than in H1, suggesting the consensus forecast of -4.2% in comparable sales in H2 appears overdone.

Furthermore, web traffic data indicates an acceleration to +9.4% in the third quarter versus -7.8% in the second quarter.

Accordingly, Citi Research raised its FY25 EPS estimate to $0.96 from $0.86 and Q3 EPS forecast to a loss of $0.10 from a loss of $0.21.

The firm also lifted its price target for Kohl’s (NYSE:KSS) by 19% to $19.

data.title: Walmart faces questions linking lax third-party vetting to marketplace counterfeits -- report
data.description:
Shares of Walmart (NYSE:WMT) are on watch in the wake of a CNBC report alleging that the retail giant’s lax vetting process for third-party merchants has resulted in a proliferation of counterfeit products on its website.

A CNBC investigation into Walmart’s (NYSE:WMT) marketplace uncovered “dozens” of third-party sellers who had stolen the identity of another business,” and subsequently sold fake health and beauty products online.

The network included an interview with a Walmart employee responsible for approving third-party applications. According to the employee, Walmart (NYSE:WMT) was initially strict on third-party applications, but over time pressured employees to relax their standards and approve more sellers due to increased competition from Amazon (AMZN).

She added that sellers who were frustrated with Amazon’s (AMZN) rigorous screening moved to Walmart (NYSE:WMT), which allowed them to list their products more easily. Data compiled by Marketplace Pulse found that during the period between 2019 and 2024, the number of sellers on Walmart’s marketplace exploded by 900%, overlapping with a period in which Amazon (AMZN) increased security controls and banned many sellers for selling counterfeits.

By accepting more risks, Walmart’s (WMT) U.S. e-commerce business has swelled to nearly $100B in annual sales, CNBC claims. “They wanted the business, so they were willing to take a chance on it,” the employee said to CNBC.

In a statement to CNBC, Walmart (WMT) said it enforces a “zero-tolerance policy for prohibited or noncompliant products and continues to invest in new tools and technologies to help ensure only trusted, legitimate items reach our customers.”

However, as part of its investigation into Walmart (WMT) and Amazon’s (AMZN) seller applications, CNBC found that Walmart (WMT) required less documentation and vetting to sign up for its marketplace and imposed fewer restrictions on the types of products that could be sold compared to its competitors like Amazon (AMZN). The network tested the authenticity of 20 items offered by third-party sellers that were impersonating legitimate businesses and found all 20 were counterfeit.

Shares of Walmart (WMT) are down nearly 1% matching a move across the consumer staples sector (XLP). ETFs with an exposure to Walmart (WMT) include iShares Global Consumer Staples (KXI), Global X Purecap MSCI Consumer Staples (GXPS), and  Vanguard Consumer Staples Index Fund (VDC).

Walmart (WMT) has not yet responded to Seeking Alpha’s request for comment.

data.title: Miran says Trump didn't pressure him on how to vote at FOMC meeting
data.description:
Stephen Miran, who was on the White House staff at the start of this week, said as a Federal Reserve governor, "I will do my own independent analysis, based on my interpretation of the data."

He spoke to President Donald Trump once since he was sworn in shortly before the Federal Open Market Committee meeting on Tuesday. The president called to congratulate him and didn't ask him to take any specific actions, Miran said on CNBC.

Miran was the only FOMC member to dissent from the Fed's decision to cut its benchmark rate by 25 basis points. Instead, he was pushing for a 50-bp cut.

He took an unpaid leave of absence from the Council of Economic Advisers when the was sworn in as a Fed governor on Tuesday. That has raised criticism from Democrats, who see it as a conflict of interest.

All Fed members were welcoming at the meeting this week, he said, including Lisa Cook, who President Trump is trying to remove from the central bank.

Miran isn't seeing "any material inflation from tariffs," but "I'm clearly in the minority" in assessing the effect of tariffs on inflation, he added. One indicator backing his view is that "there's no discernable trend difference between core goods inflation in the United States vs. that in other countries." 

He expects that less immigration and the immigrants leaving the U.S. will have a disinflationary effect, as there will be less demand, especially for housing.

The new FOMC member provided the most dovish outlook in the Summary of Economic Projections' dot plot, where he projected the federal funds rate at between 2.75% and 3.00% at the end of 2025. Miran said he'll deliver a speech on Monday that will give "a full accounting" of his economic views, laying out the math for how he gets to those numbers.

If the interest rate goes down, then government debt cost will decline, he noted. But Miran said he'll focus on the Fed's dual mandate of full employment and stable prices.

When asked if the Fed should use its balance sheet to bring down mortgage rates, "I don't think it's appropriate for the Fed to be involved in credit allocation," he said.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Brighthouse Financial gains after report of Aquarian $65-$70/share offer
data.description:
Brighthouse Financial (NASDAQ:BHF) rose 6% after a report that a consortium led by insurer Aquarian Holdings is in advanced discussions to pay $65 to $70 a share for Brighthouse.

Brighthouse (NASDAQ:BHF) may decide as soon as next week to go forward with a sale or to remain a standalone company, according to a Bloomberg report on  Friday, which cited people familiar with the matter. Aquarian is working with co-investors including Mubadala Capital and the Qatar Investment Authority.

Brighthouse declined to comment to Bloomberg. Aquarian didn’t immediately respond to request for comment. 

The latest update comes after the Financial Times reported earlier this month that Aquarian was said to be in late-stage discussions  with investors for financing its takeover of the Brighthouse (BHF).

data.title: Market Voices: Xi on TikTok, Fed's Kashkari, SEC chair on rule change
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's roundup of statements, announcements and remarks that could impact markets, sectors or individual stocks.

  • Chinese President Xi Jinping told President Donald Trump during a call on Friday to discuss trade policy that his government was open to negotiating over TikTok, but didn't indicate a deal had already been reached.

 "On TikTok, Xi said China's position is clear: the Chinese government respects corporate will and welcomes companies to conduct business negotiations on the basis of market rules to reach a solution consistent with Chinese laws and regulations and a balance of interests," according to Reuters, which cited Chinese state media outlet Xinhua. 

TikTok is owned by China's ByteDance (BDNCE). 

  • Minneapolis Federal Reserve President Neel Kashkari said he doesn't expect tariffs to significantly pressure inflation long term, adding that he hopes to see the Fed vote for additional rate cuts at its too remaining meetings this year.  

"It really comes down to, do you believe tariffs are a one-time effect or something more persistent?” Kashkari said during an interview with CNBC on Friday. “I’m getting more confident that it’s likely a one-time effect, but it’s going to take a couple years for it to play out.” 

Kashkari is not currently a voting member of the Federal Open Market Committee, but will become one next year, CNBC added. 

  • SEC Chairman Paul Atkins said he plans to propose that companies be given the option of filing financial reports biannually rather than quarterly. 

Atkin's announcement comes in the wake of comments by President Trump that quarterly reporting was an unnecessary drain on company resources. 

“I welcome that posting by the president, and I have talked to him about it,” Atkins told CNBC on Friday. “In principle, I think to propose change in what our rules are now, I think would be a good way forward, and then we’ll consider that and move forward after that.” 

If the SEC approves the change, it would be up to individual companies to decide whether they want to report quarterly or biannually, he added. 

“For the sake of shareholders and public companies, the market can decide what the proper cadence is,” Atkins said.

data.title: Quantum computing stocks rise to end action-packed week
data.description:
Quantum computing stocks rose on Friday to end an action-packed week in the sector. 

Leading the gains were Quantum Co. (NASDAQ:QMCO), which rose more than 28% in late morning trading, and Quantum Computing (NASDAQ:QUBT), which added more than 22%. Also seeing strong gains were IonQ (NYSE:IONQ), Arqit Quantum (NASDAQ:ARQQ) and D-Wave Quantum (QBTS), all of which rose 3% or more. Rigetti Computing (RGTI) added nearly 11%, shortly before 12 p.m. EST.

It was unclear what was driving the moves higher in the sector, though investor interest in the space has been renewed with vigor in 2025, amid advancements and tech giants such as Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT) and Nvidia (NVDA) showing an interest.

Earlier this week, IonQ, Honeywell (HON) and The Electric Power Board of Chattanooga partnered with the U.S. Department of Energy to boost quantum computing use cases in space. IonQ also said it was acquiring Vector Atomic to expand into quantum sensing.
